speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 25, 1996. We will be interviewing Alfred Traum. My name is Amy Godin. We are in Silver Spring, Maryland, USA. The language is English. Today is December 25, 1996. We will be interviewing Alfred Traum. My name is Amy Godin. We are located in Silver Spring, Maryland, USA. The language is English. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Alfred Traum, known to my friends as Freddie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born in March 22, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever gone by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Elias Traum, and my mother was Gita, also called [NON-ENGLISH] Traum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have a sister, Ruth Doniger now. She lives in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What business was your father in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a, a business of kind of selling on [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] high-purchase. I'm not sure what you call it, where people would pay off the goods before you could do this sort of thing in the store. We would buy, and they, they would pay off over a period of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father was invalided. As best of my recollection, he was crippled from the day I can remember him. But it was due to being in the trenches in the First World War. I guess it was rheumatic fever or rheumatoid arthritis or something like that. But-- so he was housebound, and he did everything from the home. And my wife-- my wife. Sorry. [LAUGHS] My mother was the outside person. So she would do the purchases and contact customers if they didn't come to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she worked in the family business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life as a child in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived-- wasn't really suburb by American standards, but we weren't in the heart of the inner city. We were living in a pretty much a non-Jewish area, although there were synagogues around there, and I used to go to services every Saturday. But my life was very assimilated in as far as my play time and the type of things I used to get up to. Played with the kids next door and the kids in the street, dressed the same way as they did and did pretty much everything else that a kid growing up in that area would do at that age. I think I had a, a happy life. I had a nice environment around me and off to camps in summer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Had an aunt in Bratislava, which is kind of close to Czechoslovakia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Used to spend some-- a couple of times-- spend summer there. So I think it was a pretty nice, happy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends and Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much so. In fact, I think at, at that time I probably had more non-Jewish friends, although we were very close with my family, my uncle. And he had four sons, and they lived in Vienna. But they lived in the other side of Vienna, which was very much a, a Jewish-- I wouldn't call it a ghetto, but it was a, a very heavily Jewish-populated area, the Second District, and they were very Orthodox. But I used to spend a great deal of time there with them, too. So we had-- I had both sides of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Secular and religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about celebrating the Jewish holidays at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a fairly traditional home, although we were not as Orthodox as my uncle's house. But as far as Friday night, it was always a traditional Shabbat meal, Kiddush, and all that sort of thing. Lighting candles. Thursday night was actually the big night where my mother used to bake everything. There was nothing shop-bought. Even noodles that go into soup were hand-rolled and laid out and dried and then cut. And our challot, they-- she was plaiting them and baking those in the oven. I never knew what a shop, shop-bought cookie was. That was something that I kind of thought must be wonderful, but I never had any. And holidays, if it was Pesach, we used to go to my uncle's, and we used to spend it there. Even though he was, as I mentioned, very religious, my dad was kind of the amateur photographer who, despite of the orthodoxy, was allowed to take a flash picture, which in those days was lighting a piece of paper and the things kind of exploding and providing the light for this time exposure. Everybody's eyes were closed except my dad's. They were wide open waiting for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's how we used to spend some of the holidays. What did your house look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived in a, in a big old apartment building. Now, many of those old apartment buildings in Vienna had entrances that were high and wide that could allow a horse and cart fully laden with all kinds of things to go through it. In the place where we lived, it was necessary to do that because way behind there, there were cowsheds and, I think, about 15 cows. And there was a dairy downstairs that sold milk. And, of course, they had traffic going in and out with food and that sort of thing. And I used to help out in this cow shed. And I used to love playing-- working with the horses, grooming them. And, and just helping out there. It was a good, good kind of pastime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a close relationship with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think so. Very close. Especially with my father, because he was there all the time. And for him to get out, we could only do that with great, a great deal of difficulty. And, you know, you know, we didn't have elevators in the, in the building. He didn't-- we didn't have a car. So it was only on special occasions. When we went somewhere, I used to have to go and-- to the taxi rank and bring a taxi home and look for one that had the lowest steps so he could get into it. It was a-- it was quite an ordeal for him. So most of the time he was at home. But he could do-- never heard him complain. He could do everything as far as making toys, making clothes, mending my shoes, drawing for me, working with my school work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was all around, and we had a wonderful relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he helped her, too. But I don't think they had quite as close a relationship as I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as a, as a little kid I think I was considered more like a thorn in her side, that occasionally she had to drag me along to places where she didn't want to or she felt I was spying on her or something. I think the usual sibling rivalry was there. But, you know, nothing, nothing more than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my first school was a elementary school. Again, this was the regular school that the kids in the street went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the neighborhood. No, I don't remember that. And everything was fine. I think I enjoyed school. When they had religion, those kids that were not Christian or Catholic, which is what that community was primarily, used leave the room. And-- but there wasn't any overt anti-Semitism that I experienced as a little kid in, in, in that school at that time. It all changed later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types of games did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Football. By football I mean soccer. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I don't know. You know, we didn't have as many implements and dressing up for everything like, like kids do these days. We used to play-- make games out of just about anything. And I think your imagination was the kind of horizon of your, of your games, and not what you could buy. I liked to ride a bike, and whenever I had any money, I used to go to the park and rent a bicycle and ride home with it. And usually my dad would be at the window watching out for me. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's-- I think that's what I spent most of my money, whenever I had any, on. It went on that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to religious school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to have religious lessons in school. And when, when the other children had their religious lessons, we had, we had ours. Things were also very organized there that we went to school half day on Saturday, and in the afternoon, we went to special religious services in the synagogue. And they used to have a printed piece of card-- in fact, I have a, a Xerox copy of one of here-- that they used to give you. This was relating to this week's portion that was being read in a synagogue. And we had to bring that to school to show that we went to synagogue and had our instruction in this way. Now, in the Orthodox environment it would have been very different for-- to begin with, they wouldn't have gone on Saturday morning to regular school. But that's the way it was with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your first recollection of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's a good question, but it's difficult to answer because these things didn't happen suddenly. You didn't suddenly have Stormtroopers banging around and knocking people around. It's subtle. I remember we had a radio at home, and some of our neighbors who were very friendly with us didn't. And Hitler's speeches were being broadcasted, and they came in to our house and listened to the speeches. And I could remember my mother feeling very upset-- and my father-- in listening to these speeches and not fully understanding what was being said but knowing that this was something bad for us. And, you know, and this happened on more than one occasions when our neighbors came in to listen. And I don't recall any discussion regarding the speeches, but they went on for hours and-- very heated type of speeches. You may have heard them on some of these shows occasionally. And so I think this was the first indication that something bad is looming up there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next thing I knew that some of my school pals were being enlisted into-- well, I think they were too young for the Hitler Youth. There was another thing called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Deutsche Jugend, DJ. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the younger kids went into that, and so--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight, nine. The same age as I was. And so they would-- everybody was getting a uniform. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a spirit, to some degree. You might say it was contagious, because, you know, everybody was busy being involved in things and bands being formed and people marching around. And I was on the outside looking in, not-- in some ways perhaps, envious. The not being part of all this new adventure. And yet, at the same time, knowing that there was something unhealthy about it as far as I was concerned. Although the kids at that time still used to come, and they used to show me what they got. And they had these little daggers, and we'd play with those things. Then on another occasion-- militarism started becoming very evident. I don't know what the occasion was, but there was a marketplace near us, and they had the military there with ack-ack guns and searchlights and all that. And, of course, all the kids went running up there and sat down on the equipment and were turning the handles and spinning around. And I was in amongst them doing exactly the same thing. You know, not feeling any-- particularly any different, just doing what kids do. Then-- but then gradually, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they stopped playing with me. Now, it may have been because of their activity level was heightened because of all these things that they were involved in. And so they-- I wasn't involved in any of that, so we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to get involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was no way I could. Being Jewish, I, I couldn't-- I wasn't allowed to be in any of these functions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that. I don't know if, if I-- anybody said to me, you can't come, or you're not-- I think this was something-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] something was well, well-known to or made well-known to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But then gradually they started shunning me. So that was the difference between just not being part of it and being kind of ostracized from the-- what was going on around me. And to the point that once, leaving school, a bunch of kids-- we were going through the park. And in Vienna the parks are, are not like they seem to be in, in the States or in England where the grass is meant to be enjoyed underfoot. There you stick to the paved roads, and the grass is just to be looked at. And some kids came along, and they ripped off my school satchel I used to wear on my back and threw it onto the grass. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I went over to retrieve it, and there was a policeman standing right by there. So he grabbed a hold of me, because I trod on the grass and made a big scene about this. Took down my name, and my parents were called to school. And the teacher made a big issue about how these Jewish kids have no respect for property and, you know, this tremendous-- big, big thing they made out of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you walked on the grass.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and just because they pushed everything of mine on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: '38. Something. A strange thing, sometimes a, a sight or a smell can bring things back to you in a rush. Years later, around 1952, I was a tank commander in the British Army going along at the end of Rhine army maneuvers, going along the little town of Hamley along the River Weser there. There are all neatly lawns there on the side of the road. And the driver, who used to ask me to translate every sign he saw, asked me, what's that sign down there? And so I said, it's 'keep off the grass.' And as soon-- soon as I saw that, boy, I was transported back in time to that issue. And I remember telling him, right straight on, and we went right over that sign and we dug a trench in that road, must have been about three feet deep. But it's, it's just an indication how that feeling of kind of frustration and, and anger can well up in you. It doesn't matter how many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So let's get back to 1938. What happened next? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then they decreed that the Jewish kids should go to school for Jews. And so the certain schools were designated for that. And so I left this school and went to another school which was quite a ways off. I had to take a tramway there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that? Was it a relief or was it a source of anxiety?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in one respect it was a relief because now I didn't have to be the only kid in a class with everybody making remarks, shouting things, calling you names, and sometimes beating you up on the outside. And, and on top of that, the teachers fostering that kind of behavior. So all that kind of thing stopped. On the other hand, you know, you suddenly realize that you're, you're different. You don't really understand why, but you are. And, of course, it was quite a long ways from my home. I didn't have any friends from there, because they all went to different-- in different directions. And as I said, I lived in this area where there were very few Jews, so therefore the-- coming back I was-- I came by myself. I used to usually ride there by tram and then walk home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents still have their business?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had it, but it got to the point where it became very difficult. Some people simply refused to pay. They said, we're not going to pay you. You know, what are you going to do? And you couldn't do anything. And so things got pretty bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd had some incidents at that school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one was kind of humorous and the other one wasn't. One was where I was going to school, and somehow that tram took off before I could get on it. And the way things were, the trams were in the middle of the road, and the, the other vehicles went on the side. So next thing I knew, I was on the floor, and a, a car hit me and knocked me off my feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I wasn't hurt. But it was one of those SA staff cars. And this officer, the SA, got out and asked me if I was hurt. So I said, no. So he said, where are you going? So I said, I'm going to school. So he said, well, jump in. I'll take you. So I got in next to him, and it was this staff car with a Nazi flag on it. Did he know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I mean, I don't think I, I look particularly Jewish. And, and there wouldn't have been any ways of knowing unless he asked me, and he didn't know the name of the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we drove up to this school and-- where all my friends and everybody were. All of the school mates were just coming in. And it was enough-- it was a bad enough feeling for anybody seeing a car like this driving up there, because that usually meant some kind of trouble. All of a sudden they saw me getting out of it. So that caused a lot of smirks and laughter in the, in the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it wasn't always like that, because I remember on one occasion-- or several occasions-- kids used to hang around outside waiting for us to come out and then either jeer or just to try and hit kids. And since I used to go in the, in the opposite direction from the majority of them, I remember there's a bunch of boys started chasing me. And I ran as fast as my legs could take me. And I dashed into a side entrance of one of those houses and hid behind those big doors I was talking about. And I must have been there for half an hour at least. And I was surprised they couldn't hear my heart beating, because it was beating so hard. And I think I wet my pants, too. [CRYING] I feel, I feel angry every time-- [CRYING] just when I think about that, that situation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But those were some of the situations we had. Now, some of the other things that went on-- to get my bicycle I had to go through a, a park [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where I weren't supposed to be there. But the only way I could get there was by going through the park. So I used to go in and rent a bicycle. And I think I, I'd like to illustrate a point which is important. On one occasion, I, I thought I'd lost my money. OK.
